section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: for State Authority in New Spain * Francisco Garfias † and Emily A. Sellars ‡ Keywords: Institutions; Historical Political Economy; State Building; Mexico Abstract We examine the shortand longer-term consequences of the expulsion of the Jesuit order from New Spain in 1767.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: The Jesuits had been critical partners of the Spanish Crown in the expansion and stabilization of colonial rule, but royal officials eventually became suspicious of the order’s economic and political power and their ability to resist imperial authority.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: Though authorities believed that expelling the Jesuits would bolster the power, resources, and authority of the colonial state, we show that it had ambiguous short-term and more unfavorable longer-term consequences on these outcomes.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: The expropriation of Jesuit assets provided a resource boost for the Crown, but the benefit was partially offset by the additional burdens of replacing Jesuit institutions and pacifying unrest caused by the expulsion.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: The legacy of 1767 proved more damaging over the longer term as affected areas saw more insurgent activity during Mexico’s War of Independence (1810–1821).

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: This case highlights both the benefits and hidden dangers of relying on religious intermediaries to extend political control.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion: A Double-Edged Sword
text: * We thank Luz Marina Arias, Kerice Doten-Snitker, Jean-Paul Faguet, John Tutino, and participants of the Santa Fe Institute Workshop on “Instrumental Incoherence in Institutional Reform” for comments on this project. † Associate Professor, School of Global Policy and Strategy, UC San Diego. e: fgarfias@ucsd.edu ‡ Assistant Professor, Department of Political Science, Yale University. e: emily.sellars@yale.edu 1 1.

section_name: Introduction
text: The relationship between religious authority and state power is complicated and often uneasy.

section_name: Introduction
text: Religion has sometimes been a useful tool for state building, allowing political authorities to co-opt the resources and legitimacy of religious institutions to extend their power and influence.

section_name: Introduction
text: On the other hand, independent organized religion also poses a unique threat to political rulers in that it provides an alternative source of authority that can be used to mobilize societal actors to challenge the state from within.

section_name: Introduction
text: As the history of European state building illustrates, the balance between these two forces can be difficult to calibrate.

section_name: Introduction
text: Religious institutions often served as key intermediaries between the state and society, but they also stood in the way of later state-building efforts, sometimes through violence.

section_name: Introduction
text: These tensions are especially evident when examining the centuries-long relationship between the Spanish Empire and the Society of Jesus, the Jesuit order of the Catholic Church.

section_name: Introduction
text: The Jesuits became critical partners in the expansion of Spanish colonial rule across the Americas through financing missions, educational institutions, churches, and even investment in the local economy.

section_name: Introduction
text: Over time, however, the independent power of the Jesuits to evade political oversight and taxation became seen as a dire threat to state authority.

section_name: Introduction
text: This conflict culminated in 1767 when Charles III announced the immediate expulsion of all Jesuit clergy from the Empire, an order that would be implemented with surprising exhaustiveness across the Americas within a few short years.

section_name: Introduction
text: In this paper, we examine the shortand longer-term consequences of the Jesuit expulsion in colonial Mexico (New Spain), a region in which the order had amassed considerable power and influence.

section_name: Introduction
text: Using a combination of qualitative and quantitative data, we provide evidence that the expulsion had ambiguous short-term and more unfavorable longer-term consequences for colonial rule.

section_name: Introduction
text: Spanish authorities gained power and resources through the expropriation of Jesuit property and the secularization of Jesuit educational and mission activities.

section_name: Introduction
text: At the same time, the expulsion sparked a wave of unrest and forced the Crown to find alternative strategies 1 for governance, education, and pacification in the absence of Jesuit mediation.

section_name: Introduction
text: The seemingly successful implementation of the expulsion order demonstrated the Crown’s resolve and capacity to execute difficult reforms, paving the way for a subsequent wave of Bourbon state-building efforts.

section_name: Introduction
text: Over the longer term, however, grievances over the expulsion among the creole elite exacerbated growing tensions with the Crown, contributing to the outbreak of the War of Independence and the eventual collapse of Spanish rule.

section_name: Introduction
text: Our findings on the Jesuit expulsion speak to a more general pattern in state building.

section_name: Introduction
text: Central rulers often rely on intermediaries—both religious organizations like the Jesuits, as well as economic elites, local notables, or traditional authorities—to administer populations, control territory, and provide legitimacy.

section_name: Introduction
text: State-building reforms that seek to consolidate more revenue and authority in the center often come at the expense of these intermediaries and their allies.

section_name: Introduction
text: In alienating important domestic interests, state building can lead to political backlash, both immediately and later on when political ties are tested during spells of state vulnerability.

section_name: Introduction
text: Like many other political reforms, the Jesuit expulsion sparked unrest in its immediate aftermath.

section_name: Introduction
text: Though authorities succeeded and repressing these initial revolts, the expulsion activated longer-lasting grievances among the Jesuit-educated elite, which, more consequentially, contributed to the escalation of conflict during a broader political crisis decades later.

section_name: Introduction
text: By focusing on the interplay between state building and religious authority, this paper contributes to a recent reexamination of the many roles that the Catholic Church played in the emergence and development of the modern state.

section_name: Introduction
text: More specifically, we build on a growing literature on Jesuit political and economic activities, particularly in colonial Spanish America. 1 Our work also relates to a much 1 Some related work focuses on the legacies of mission activity in Africa and Asia (e.g., Gallego and Woodberry 2 broader literature on the often complex connection between religious authorities and the state in other contexts, which draws attention to many of the same ambiguities highlighted here.

section_name: Introduction
text: Finally, our results add to a growing literature in historical political economy that focuses on colonial rule in the Americas.

section_name: Introduction
text: Most specifically, this work builds on recent scholarship on the Bourbon Reforms, a transformative wave of reforms that included the Jesuit expulsion as part of what Brading characterized as a “revolution in government” reshaping the colonial state. 2.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: As the Spanish Empire extended its political influence across the Western Hemisphere, it was confronted with a classic set of governance challenges.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Royal authorities in Iberia could not project power across this distant, unknown, and expansive territory on their own.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: They had to rely heavily on the cooperation of local intermediaries—including conquistadors, economic elites, Indigenous authorities, and Catholic missionaries, among others—to consolidate political control and administer territory on their behalf.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: As in other colonial and frontier contexts, these local intermediaries were better positioned to take on the day-to-day tasks of governance given their presence and status in the colony.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Those intermediaries who commanded respect or authority independently of the Spanish state could further assist by lending their independent political legitimacy to the project of imperial expansion, as did some aligned members of the Indigenous nobility, for example. 2010; Nunn 2010; Bai and Kung 2015; Fenske 2015; Wantchekon, Klašnja, and Novta 2015; Cagé and Rueda 2016; Bergeron 2020; Okoye 2021).

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Religious authorities and institutions were particularly useful partners in the process of state expansion and consolidation.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: From the scale of local clergy up to supranational organizations (e.g., the Catholic hierarchy centered in the Vatican), religious actors are often uniquely well suited to act as intermediaries between state actors and society.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Religious authorities may enjoy a certain independent legitimacy among the local population, which can be leveraged to foster belief in or compliance with state institutions and activities.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Beyond these cultural or symbolic advantages, there may be direct material benefits to farming out administrative tasks to religious actors or institutions who can fund their activities via non-state sources of financing, whether tithes from the local population or transfers from higher-level religious organizations.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: The constellation of pre-existing institutions associated with organized religion can also be altered, co-opted, or copied in the process of state building to take on new administrative tasks, such as local record-keeping, education, or taxation.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Entrepreneurial state builders have formed strategic alliances with religious authorities across many places and times, from medieval Europe to post-Ottoman Turkey.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: However, as these and other examples illustrate, there is an underlying risk to such an alliance as well.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: The interests of state and religious authorities are not always aligned.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: When conflicts between these groups arise, the same features that make religious actors uniquely valuable partners to the state can make them uniquely threatening rivals.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Religious authorities often retain a great deal of leverage in their dealings with the state due to their independent legitimacy, status, and resources.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: If pushed, they can weaponize this leverage to block or undermine new political efforts that they oppose.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Religious authorities can pose a unique threat to the state, by potentially leveraging both external ties (e.g., the Vatican or religious leaders abroad) and local influence to challenge state power, whether directly or indirectly.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: There is therefore an important tension or “incongruity” (Faguet TBD) inherent in institutional partnerships between the state and religious authorities.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Such partnerships can serve to extend and 4 enhance state power, particularly in contexts of otherwise weak state capacity.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: However, they also limit the autonomy of the state to defy the will of religious authorities going forward.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: When the interests of religious and state authorities diverge, longstanding institutional arrangements can prove difficult to renegotiate or roll back because of the independent power, status, and legitimacy of religious intermediaries.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: As we describe in the following sections, the Spanish Crown’s long-term partnership with the Jesuit order in New Spain illustrates this dilemma well.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: The Jesuits were critical partners in the growth and consolidation of the Spanish colonial state, but their political interests did not always align with those of the Crown.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: After more than a century of limited success in rolling back the order’s status and influence, Charles III abruptly expelled the Jesuits from the Empire in 1767.

section_name: Religious Intermediaries and the State
text: Far from resolving the institutional incongruities of Jesuit intermediation, the expulsion had ambiguous consequences for state power, creating some benefits for the Crown but also exposing it to new political risks that, in time, proved consequential. 3.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Society of Jesus, or the Jesuit Order, is a religious order of the Catholic Church founded around the height of the Counter-Reformation during the 16th century.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuit missionaries first arrived to New Spain in the early 1570s, about 50 years after the fall of Tenochtitlan and the formal establishment of a colonial administration, quickly becoming important social, economic, and political actors.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Christian conversion had been an important justification of the Spanish colonial project from its outset, especially as applied to areas like New Spain with sizable Indigenous populations.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: More broadly, the Catholic faith was central to the political culture and functioning of the Spanish Empire.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Ecclesiastical institutions formed one of the five coequal branches of the colonial government—the others being civil, judicial, military, and fiscal ( hacienda )—highlighting the tight link between church and state in this context.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Church’s powers extended well beyond regulating religious life.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Church maintained a parallel judicial system (in addition to the Inquisition), and religious 5 authorities often negotiated explicitly with the Crown on issues related to civil governance and taxation.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Religious authorities held positions of power in civil government as well.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As Gerhard notes, ten bishops and archbishops would become viceroys of New Spain (p. 17).

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Ecclesiastical institutions in New Spain were further subdivided into the diocesan (or “secular”) and regular religious establishments.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The former was made up of the diocesan clergy, headed by bishops and, as of the 1540s, an archbishop seated in Mexico City.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The latter was made up of clergy from the regular religious orders—in the early years of the colony, these were principally Franciscans, Augustinians, and Dominicans—each of which had separate hierarchies and (often overlapping) provincial divisions.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Upon their arrival to the Americas, the Jesuits became important players in the regular ecclesiastical establishment alongside the traditional mendicant orders.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: To understand the eventual importance of Jesuit culture and institutions to the political economy of colonial Mexico, it is worth highlighting a few different channels through which their influence was felt. 3.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuit Institutions A first important channel of Jesuit influence was in education.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The first Jesuits to arrive in Mexico City in 1572 came to establish a college to provide education to wealthy elites in the capital.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Over the next several decades, the Jesuits would found a series of schools and colleges ( colegios ), not just in Mexico City but in provincial capitals and commercial centers across the territory, including Guadalajara, Puebla, Oaxaca, Querétaro, San Luis Potosí, and Zacatecas.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: These colleges often mixed religious education with training in classical subjects like science and philosophy, some eventually receiving university status and offering training in specific faculties like medicine and law.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The network of Jesuit colleges grew to play a dominant role in educating the creole elite of New Spain up until the time of the expulsion in 1767.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As time went on, these elites became a major source of funding for the Church and began to push for the creation of additional Jesuit 6 colleges and churches in economic centers like Guanajuato.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuits also funded many primary schools, for both Spanish-speaking and Indigenous populations, extending their educational influence to other segments of society as well.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: A related but distinct role played by the Jesuits in New Spain (and across the Americas) was as missionaries to a largely Indigenous population.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As relative latecomers to the continent, the Jesuits arrived after the “spiritual conquest” of central Mexico had largely been completed and as the northward push of Spanish influence was well underway.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuits grew to carve out a dominant sphere of influence in the northwest frontier of New Spain (Nueva Vizcaya, Sinaloa, Sonora, and the Californias) and in Nayarit.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: This geography was not accidental.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: These were remote areas along and across the Western Sierra Madre mountains where the imposition of Spanish rule had been challenging.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: For the Jesuits, the remoteness was an asset rather than a liability.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: This was not just because of a religious commitment to serving the underserved, but because the distance shielded them from interference by royal officials and rival religious groups, allowing them more autonomy to carry out their activities.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: These activities included not just the Catholic conversion of the Indigenous population but also the (often forcible) reorganization of local communities, the provision of technical training, and investment in local agricultural production.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Closer to the colonial center, Jesuits also created urban “missions” to serve the local workforce in places like Guanajuato, often at the behest of local elites who sought to exert a “civilizing” influence over a population that was perceived as “unruly” and “lacking Christian commitments”.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: A last critical channel of Jesuit influence was economic.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: To pay for their educational, religious, and missionary activities, the Jesuits required funding.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Elite benefactors were an important income source, but the successful of colleges and missions required a more stable stream of resources than might be provided by charity alone.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The solution to this problem came through investing in the incipient commercial economy of New Spain, particularly through the acquisition and management 7 of large, for-profit agricultural estates ( haciendas ).

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Though their superiors in Europe expressed misgivings about this strategy at first, Mexico’s Jesuits gradually amassed huge tracts of valuable land in central and northern Mexico and used large numbers of workers, including both Indigenous laborers and enslaved Africans.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuits were not the only religious order to invest in commercial agriculture—the Franciscans, for example, obtained and managed numerous estates across the north—but the Jesuits gained a reputation for being unusually astute investors and managers of their properties.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The economic impact in the colony was considerable.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As Tutino describes, Jesuit agricultural production worked to critically support the mining economy, making Jesuits “key participants” in Mexico’s silver boom of the early eighteenth century (p. 25). 3.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuits as Political Actors and Intermediaries Though relative latecomers to Mexico, the Jesuits grew to occupy a central place in colonial society.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As religious authorities in a Catholic empire, the Jesuits enjoyed a certain independent legitimacy in their dealings with local administrators and royal officials.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: In addition to this symbolic status, the Jesuits became important economic players in their own right through their agricultural and financial activities.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As we discuss in the next section, this special status did not insulate the Jesuits from periodic conflicts with the Crown, particularly over questions related to taxation and imperial jurisdiction.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: However, perhaps the most important source of political influence that the Jesuits possessed, and the feature of their influence that would prove hardest for officials to overcome in the wake of the expulsion, was their social embeddedness as intermediaries between the Crown and different segments of colonial society.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: When it came to elites, the Jesuits became the dominant providers of education in much of the colony, often at little to no direct cost to the Crown.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The symbolic importance of Jesuit education lent the order a certain legitimacy that could be leveraged to secure support for the colonial state more generally or, as we discuss later, to undermine support for the state after the expulsion.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The tight link between creole elites and the Jesuit order extended beyond education.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Creole elites became major funders and organizers of Jesuit church building, charity activities, and missionary activities.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: More broadly, Jesuits became known as “vocal champions of the creole elite” when they came into tension with peninsulares (Spanish-born elites) and the Crown, standing up against growing “European ethnocentrism” in colonial society.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Even after tensions with the Crown began to build in the eighteenth century, officials recognized the political importance of the Jesuits’ cultural linkage with the creole elite in sustaining political order, a concern that would come up in high-level discussions over the implementation of the expulsion decree.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuits acted as partners in maintaining political order among commoners as well.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As mentioned earlier, the incorporation and pacification of workers was a central justification of the network of urban Jesuit “missions” funded by elites in economic centers like Guanajuato.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: In rural areas, Jesuit haciendas functioned not just as economic enterprises but as multipurpose institutions that also provided religious education, training, and social services to resident and non-resident workers.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: As Tutino describes, the Jesuits did not always live up to their religious ideals in their work as estate managers, perhaps most obviously when it came to their reliance on slavery, but Jesuit writings at the time reflect an interest in balancing their material and spiritual responsibilities.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: An eighteenth-century document entitled Instructions for Jesuit brothers managing estates emphasizes the importance of making sure that estate workers were clothed and fed, lived in accordance with Christian morality, attended church, behaved well, avoided drunkenness, and eschewed violence.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuit estate managers would enforce these standards through regular oversight and punishment of violations, but they also relied on the voluntary compliance of the population given the order’s position and legitimacy.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuit’s role as intermediaries between the local population and the Crown was perhaps most overt with respect to the Indigenous groups of the northwest frontier.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Because of their willingness to live in remote territories and their purported “superiority in native languages” relative to other religious orders, the Jesuits were particularly important agents of the Crown in these frontier zones 9.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Civil authorities were seldom present or effective purveyors of order in these areas, and so “the church was the state, its ministers acting as judges and as representatives of the monarchy”.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: By contracting with the Jesuits to act as their stand-in, colonial officials could project power into distant territories and among populations that might have otherwise been hostile.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: One illustration of how this arrangement could work is the long-term relationship between the Jesuits and the Yaqui in the territory of Sonora.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Following years of conflict with Spanish forces, the Yaqui invited Jesuit missionaries into their territory in the early seventeenth century.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuit missionaries built churches and schools, invested in the local economy, and worked to organize the population into settlements, “exercis[ing] a near-monopoly of colonial power” in the region.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: This coincided with a century of peace between the Yaqui and the Spanish, a peace that would be punctured only when direct Spanish incursion increased in the eighteenth century, instigating a massive rebellion.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: It is important not to overstate the sincerity or depth of the connection between Jesuit missionaries and the Indigenous population.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: This was a complicated and often coercive relationship, and some groups, like the Tepehuanes or Rarámuri/Tarahumara, violently and successfully resisted Jesuit efforts to make inroads for decades.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The connection between the Jesuits and mission populations frequently entailed coercion as well as protection, and the Jesuits could leverage their control over these populations to extend their own political and economic power as the comparative case of Paraguay, for example, illustrates.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Setting aside the longstanding debates over Jesuit missionaries’ true motivations, what is clear is that these missionaries’ willingness to live among Indigenous populations and speak local languages made them valuable partners in extending the reach of Spanish rule.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: When and where they could operate, Jesuit missions were frequently the only meaningful connection between many frontier populations and the colonial state.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Costs and Benefits of Jesuit Mediation Jesuit actors and institutions thus played an important role in the functioning of colonial rule in Mexico for reasons both direct and indirect.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuits controlled substantial wealth and occupied an important social position in colonial society, but their influence was also felt via their strong formal and informal ties to elites and commoners in different parts of the territory and across different types of institutions.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: From the above discussion, it is perhaps easy to see why Spanish authorities came to rely so heavily on the Jesuits in extending and stabilizing colonial rule.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: By inviting Jesuit clergy to mediate the connection between the colonial population and the Crown, authorities made it easier to pacify and incorporate local populations, provide makeshift administrative and religious institutions along the frontier, and generate surplus for the local economy.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Moreover, by facilitating the Jesuits’ educational and missionary activities in New Spain, the Crown could reap the symbolic and material benefits of a closer connection with the Vatican, which was itself no small concern for a Catholic Empire.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: However, this imperial strategy was not without risk.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: The Jesuits occupied a unique position because of of their political and social status.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Jesuit clergy in principle answered to a different (arguably higher) authority than the monarch himself: their Jesuit superiors in Europe and the Vatican.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: If and when they came in conflict with civil authorities, Jesuit clergy could invoke these external connections to push back on any imperial efforts to curtail their influence.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Perhaps even more ominously, the Jesuits also commanded a great deal of independent legitimacy and respect from within the colonial state because of their critical role as intermediaries.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: Over time, the very characteristics that made the Jesuits such useful allies in the Spanish colonial project also made them uniquely threatening as potential subversives, especially as the philosophy and structure of Spanish rule began to change in the second half of the eighteenth century.

section_name: The Jesuits in New Spain
text: At that point, the underlying fragility and “incongruities” of the alliance between Jesuits and the colonial state became apparent (Faguet TBD).

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The rapid and near-complete expulsion of the Jesuits from the Spanish Empire in 1767 remains one of the more mysterious events in imperial history.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Charles III’s announcement of the expulsion came unexpectedly.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The precise justification was left vague, with the king citing “urgent, just and compelling causes” that would remain concealed in the “royal breast” for eternity.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In truth, while the severity of the expulsion order, and the speed with which it was carried out in the Americas, came as a surprise to many, tensions had been building between the Jesuits and the Crown long before 1767.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This episode was also far from the first instance of direct conflict between church and state involving the Jesuit order.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: To understand the political consequences of the expulsion, it is important to review these underlying conflicts. 4.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: External Determinants Some reasons for the expulsion had little to do with New Spain, the Americas, or colonial rule.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In the years leading up to 1767, the Jesuit order had found itself under increasing suspicion across Europe.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: At the time of Charles III’s decision, the Jesuits had already been expelled from France and Portugal for “reasons of state”. Austria and Hungary would soon follow suit.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Within a decade, Pope Clement XIV would issue a papal brief from Rome suppressing the order continent-wide.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: There were doctrinal conflicts behind the suppression of the order—perhaps the most famous of which was the growing cleavage with “Jansenism” in eighteenth-century France—but the reasons behind the decision were also unmistakably political.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Jesuits had been active in politics, at times leveraging their symbolic position and connection to Rome to resist or challenge royal authority.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: They became seen as a potential enemy living within the state, an impression that was not always without foundation.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In Iberia, the Jesuits had backed the rebellion by the Duke of Braganza against the Spanish Crown in 1640, beginning a conflict that eventually led to the dissolution of the Iberian Union.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Tensions with the Crown had only grown over the eighteenth century as the Bourbon Crown sought to curtail the influence of 12 regular religious orders, often in the face of Jesuit resistance.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The immediate impetus behind Charles III’s decision is sometimes cited as the Esquilache (or “Hat-and-Cloak”) Riots in Madrid in 1766, when an angry mob took to the streets in response to reforms discouraging the wearing of traditional Castilian clothes.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Rioters eventually entered the royal palace, forcing the king to take refuge and eventually to remove the Marquis of Esquilache, the author of the hated reforms, as Secretary of the Treasury.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: A fiscal of the Council of Castile, Pedro de Campomanes, wrote a lengthy report blaming the Jesuits for serving as the secret organizing force behind this incident, denouncing the order as “the enemy of the Sovereign Power, depending on a despotic government resident in a foreign country,” in reference to the Vatican and Rome.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Campomanes was far from alone in his suspicions.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In 1766, the Count de la Villanueva, also on the Council of Castile, wrote a scathing report condemning the Jesuits’ legal battle with the Crown over the collection of tithes, remarking with scorn that “a subject litigates with his king, a Jesuit with his lord, Ferdinand VI [Charles III’s predecessor]”. Though mystery remains over Charles III’s decision, considerable pressure for the expulsion appears to have come from within the Castilian elite. 2 The extension of the expulsion order to New Spain is often seen as a direct outgrowth of events in Europe.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: John Tutino, for example, writes that “political reasons little connected to New Spain” were behind the expulsion, while Alan Knight casts the decision to enforce the expulsion order across Mexico as a “misguidedly doctrinaire application of peninsular policies to colonial problems”. That said, events in the Americas featured heavily in Iberian debates over the Jesuit question, including in the Campomanes report, which devoted disproportionate attention to Jesuit power in Spanish America.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Clair Segurado notes, the expulsion itself could not have happened without support and cooperation within the colony. 2 See also Stein and Stein, who conclude that “the attack on the Jesuits absolved those whose complicity in the [riots] was prompted by opposition to Esquilache’s reform program” within Iberia (p. 106).

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Economic Conflicts By the eighteenth century, the Jesuits controlled a substantial amount of wealth in New Spain, much of it lightly taxed.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Their property included a networks of missions, colleges, churches, and schools, as well as numerous large and seemingly highly profitable estates across central and northern Mexico.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: There was a long history of tension with the Crown and local authorities in Mexico over the taxation of Jesuit property and agricultural production.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Going back to the sixteenth century, the Jesuits had successfully obtained a series of papal bulls exempting them from paying the standard tithe of ten percent to the Crown.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: When the Crown moved to revoke this privilege in the early seventeenth century, Jesuits in the Americas resisted and legally challenged the basis for the decision.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In the 1640s, these tensions spilled over into a massive conflict with the Bishop of Puebla, Juan de Palafox y Mendoza, who sought to levy taxes on Jesuit production, only to be forced out of office after the viceroy sided with the Jesuits and threatened to have the bishop arrested.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This series of events was later emphasized by Campomanes in his report on the political danger of Jesuit influence.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Council of Indies formally resolved in favor of the Crown in 1655 after decades of litigation, but the Jesuits continued to resist payment, arguing that it was beyond the authority of the Crown to collect any such tax.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Jesuits finally recognized the Crown’s right to collect taxes on their property nearly a century later in 1750, reaching an arrangement as part of a larger settlement that also included meaningful concessions back to the order.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The 1750 settlement allowed for the taxation of production on Jesuits estates, but it reduced the tax rate from the standard one-tenth to one-thirtieth of the assessed value.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In what was arguably an even bigger concession, authorities agreed to shield the reports of managers and overseers on Jesuit estates from official scrutiny, allowing for greater tax avoidance.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The 1750 settlement was perceived to be so favorable to the order that there were accusations that Jesuit leaders had colluded to unduly influence Ferdinand VI through his father-confessor, the Jesuit Francisco de Rávago.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Because Jesuits’ property remained lightly taxed and poorly monitored, the Crown remained somewhat in the dark over the actual value of their estates, colleges, and missions.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Historians today continue to debate the actual value of Jesuit holdings as of 1767, though there is some consensus that the Crown probably overestimated this value, perhaps significantly, in the run up to the expulsion.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The lack of transparency over Jesuit holdings allowed rumors to propagate about the vast “riches” controlled by the order, which further fed anti-Jesuit sentiment and served as a motivation for the later seizure of Jesuits’ assets. 4.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Political Conflicts The conflicts between the Jesuits and the colonial state went beyond taxation.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As in Europe, there had been a gradual trend toward rolling back the autonomy and privileges of the regular religious orders in New Spain in favor of diocesan clergy.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This was not just for economic reasons but also political ones.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The regular orders existed under a separate political hierarchy than diocesan clergy and answered to a separate set of authorities: their superiors in Europe, not the archbishop.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This distinction could create tensions with the Crown and the diocesan religious establishment.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: A key question underlying conflicts between the Jesuits and royal officials in the Americas was whether local authorities had any actual jurisdiction over the order.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Though the Jesuits eventually conceded that the Crown held “full dominion” to tax their estates, they refused to recognize the authority of the Council of Indies or American bishops, opting to settle the issue directly with the king and his ministers in Madrid.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: To resolve another set of conflicts over autonomy, the Jesuits went above the Crown to secure a papal brief in 1766 reinforcing Jesuit missionaries’ rights to read books banned by the Inquisition, issue marriage dispensations, and declare local Indigenous populations as “neophytes” that could be kept on missions (and thus outside the king’s direct jurisdiction) indefinitely.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This brief undercut the authority of local bishops, the Inquisition, and the civil administration to constrain the Jesuits’ activities, so much so that 15 Charles III ordered the Council of Indies to ban the document from circulation.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Minister of Indies complained over “the disparity with which that Court [in Rome] treats our bishops of the Indies as compared to the Jesuits, since what to the former it concedes with so much difficulty, to the latter it dispenses with unparalleled freedom”.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The power the Jesuits could command outside the colonial state was troubling to the Crown, but what was perhaps more alarming was the power that Jesuit clergy had to resist the state from within.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Jesuits forged close connections with different segments of the population in New Spain through their work as educators, missionaries, clergy, and even employers.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As mentioned earlier, these connections had proved quite useful to the state, allowing the Jesuits to act as intermediaries between the Crown and distant populations.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: When the Jesuits found themselves at odds with the state, however, they could leverage these connections to undermine the Crown.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The conflict between the Jesuits and Bishop Palafox provides an illustration.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: When Palafox attempted to curtail the influence of the order and levy taxes on Jesuit properties in the 1640s, he found himself opposed not just by the Jesuit clergy but a wide swath of other colonial interests, eventually including even the viceroy.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In the end, his proposed reforms were unsuccessful, and Palafox was forced out of his position and back to Spain.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The backlash to another set of ill-fated reforms provides another example.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Following a series of unpopular efforts to tighten the Crown’s economic control over creole elites, a riot broke out in Mexico City in 1624.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The Jesuits joined the crowd against the viceroy, eventually contributing to the latter’s escape and removal.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: It was these two factors together—Jesuits’ ability to appeal to authority outside the colonial state and their ability to command social power within it—that made the Jesuits particularly threatening.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: There were growing worries that the Jesuits could use their position to undermine Spanish authority or strike deals with imperial rivals in places like northwest Mexico, where Jesuit missionaries held a virtual monopoly on political power.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As in Iberia, these tensions became especially prominent as the Bourbon Crown sought to expand its political control 16 over the course of the eighteenth century.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As Brading summarizes, “by reason of their corporate independence, their extensive jurisdiction, their great riches and frequent laxity.[the Church] presented a major obstacle to the plans of the Bourbon ministers to augment the power of the colonial state” (p. 7).

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Pressure for the Jesuit expulsion may have originated in Europe, but the swift application of the order to the Americas was not incidental. 4.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Implementation of the Expulsion Though tensions had been building for some time, Charles III’s expulsion order came as a shock.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: At the time, Jesuit institutions were operating without any clear expectation of imminent seizure.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In fact, leading up to 1767, the Jesuits had completed several new colleges, missions, and churches across Mexico thanks to a recent silver boom, including the famous Templo de la Compañía in Guanajuato in 1765.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As Charles III’s order came down through the colony, these activities were halted virtually overnight, and Jesuit clergy were forced to return to the continent.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: This was extraordinarily disruptive.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The forcible removal of clergy gave rise to some famous set pieces of the expulsion in popular culture, including the images of elderly Jesuit teachers being forced to pack up their belongings overnight, expelled clergy asking to pray to Our Lady of Guadalupe in Tepeyac on their way to board ships in Veracruz, or Jesuit missionaries asking to hold one last mass before departing California.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Local officials implementing the expulsion knew that they would face backlash.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Upon hearing of the expulsion plan, the viceroy warned the Minister of the Indies to expect resistance from the colonial elite as “all the clergy and lawyers, since they belong entirely to them [the Jesuits] are also the most resentful”. Officials timed the takeover of the main house of the Company of Jesus in Mexico City to coincide with the Feast of St.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: John the Baptist to reduce the possibility of immediate resistance.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Concern over elite subterfuge proved well-founded when a major conspiracy of high-placed elites in Mexico City sought to undermine the authority of inspector general José de Gálvez to implement the expulsion order, though their effort backfired.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: As we describe in the next section, the order also sparked a 17 damaging wave of popular unrest along the northern frontier and across central Mexico.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The expulsion posed a risk to the Crown, but it also created an opportunity.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: After more than a century of conflicts over tithes and taxation, the Jesuits’ network of properties and estates could be fully expropriated and sold.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: Longstanding disputes over the jurisdiction of Jesuit missions in the frontier could be settled overnight.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: The closure of Jesuit colleges could bring an immediate stop to looming educational reforms that the Crown increasingly viewed as dangerous.

section_name: The Jesuit Expulsion
text: In the next sections, we consider how these risks and opportunities resolved in the decades after. 5.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Material Windfall for the Spanish Crown Following the Jesuit expulsion, the Crown seized the order’s assets.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Having expected to unearth vast riches—Campomanes had characterized Jesuit wealth in New Spain as “exorbitant” —officials were disappointed to find both that Jesuits’ holdings were somewhat more modest and that this wealth was mostly held in the form of real estate and other durable assets rather than in a large stockpile of silver as had been rumored.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Gálvez, the inspector general who led the implementation of the expulsion order, was so convinced that the Jesuits were hiding treasure that he ordered officials to dredge the cesspool of the order’s main house in Mexico City, an effort that yielded little but “an unbearable smell in the house and raging dispositions on the part of commissioners,” as one Jesuit priest reported.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though slow to materialize and lower than expected, royal revenues from seized Jesuit properties eventually grew, reaching substantial levels by the turn of the 19th century.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The revenues and direct costs from the expulsion were organized under a separate fiscal branch known as temporalidades.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: To organize the transfer and sale of expropriated Jesuit properties, the Crown created and staffed a new bureaucracy.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: A first set of officials were hastily put in charge of implementing the expulsion order and expropriating Jesuit assets.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Many of the same officials were tasked with coordinating 18 the continuation of religious services and instruction in Jesuit schools, as well as the inventorying, classification, and valuation of seized Jesuit assets.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In 1769, regional boards staffed by top officials were established to oversee the sale and transfer of properties, as well as a number of subordinate boards to help with implementation.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In New Spain, the superior board included the viceroy, the head of the Audiencia, and the archbishop, which speaks to its political importance.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: A portion of the newly acquired revenue was set aside to pay these officials and their staff (de Fonseca and de Urrutia 1853, V p. 111).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Many formerly Jesuit schools continued to receive financial support from the agricultural estates that had been seized from the Jesuits and were now managed by the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The temporalidades also covered expenses related to the repression of unrest in the aftermath of the expulsion (de Fonseca and de Urrutia 1853, V p. 114).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Despite this bureaucratic investment and reorganization, revenues from the sale of Jesuit properties were slow to materialize.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Figure 1 plots the yearly revenue across all Royal Treasuries in New Spain that was recorded as part of the temporalidades branch.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This likely represents an incomplete account of the resources that the Crown derived from Jesuit assets prior to 1784 as the temporalidades had been managed through a distinct accounting system separate from the main tax administration until that year (de Fonseca and de Urrutia 1853, V pp. 157–158).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The data also do not capture any other strategies the Crown used to generate funds from the expropriation, such as through securing loans against accumulated assets.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In 1783, these loans reached 2. million pesos, a significant amount.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As the figure illustrates, the revenue recorded as part of the temporalidades branch of the tax administration did not become significant until well into the 1780s.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The accounting issues mentioned earlier contributed to this delay, as did the sizable expenses of the expulsion, which reduced the net funds reaching the Treasury.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: For example, as of 1784, only 3 of 21 Jesuit schools had completed a full accounting and redistribution of ecclesiastical decorations to other orders (de Fonseca and de Urrutia 1853, V p. 178), and the Crown did not order the sale of many of these decorations until the 1780s.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Some valuable Jesuit properties, such as missions, were simply transferred to other regular orders or diocesan clergy, in part to guarantee the continuity of religious services and to reassure the Church that the expulsion had been narrowly directed at the Jesuits.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The sale of Jesuit estates proceeded especially slowly, not only because the high value of these assets severely reduced the pool of potential buyers, but also because the process was at times deliberately delayed by officials to facilitate rent-seeking.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In the wealthy silver-producing region of Zacatecas, for example, two of the three Jesuit 20 haciendas were finally purchased by one of New Spain’s magnates, Pedro Romero de Terreros, more than a decade later in 1781, but his death shortly thereafter delayed payment as his estate underwent settlement.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Auctions of Jesuits’ other holdings in Zacatecas, including urban properties, did not begin until 1785, eighteen years after the expulsion order.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As late as 1802, a significant portion of expropriated Jesuit properties remained unsold.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Over time, the Crown began to put more pressure on officials to resolve these issues.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These efforts picked up following the end of the Anglo-Spanish War in 1783, when the Crown sought to cover its expenses by tapping into this source of wealth.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: An edict in 1784 ordered the liquidation of all Jesuit assets, while another in 1786 directed officials to reduce the valuation of Jesuit goods to enable their final auction (de Fonseca and de Urrutia 1853, V p. 165).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As the figure shows, temporalidades revenue recorded in the Treasury began to increase in the late 1780s and remained substantial for the next decades.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: After 1790, revenue from Jesuit properties represents about 1. percent of total treasury revenue, surpassing 3 percent in some years.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though this figure is smaller than New Spain’s major sources of revenue like silver mining or the alcabala tax—corresponding to about 15 percent and 4. percent of total revenue respectively—it is of comparable magnitude to the tributo, the Indigenous capitation tax (2. percent), which is also represented in the figure for scale. 3 Some of these funds had to be used to cover expenses related to the expulsion, such as a sizable pension that the Crown provided to the exiled Jesuits.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: However, the revenue generated from temporalidades in New Spain alone was more than sufficient to cover the costs incurred across the 3 As the figure reflects, there was a significant increase in recorded capitation tax revenue over this period as well.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We do not suggest that this increase was driven by the Jesuit expulsion.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This shift may instead be related to the demographic expansion in the second half of the eighteenth century or, alternatively, to a series of fiscal and administrative reforms that strengthened overall tax collection and improved imperial record-keeping during this period.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though the secularization of Jesuit missions in principle made former residents of mission communities subject to the tax, this change affected only a small number of individuals, and effective tax collection remained limited in most of these frontier areas (Section 5.3).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: 21 entire hemisphere. 4 This left a nontrivial surplus for the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In short, though there were challenges in managing the transfer and sale of Jesuit properties, and while some of this wealth was lost to other religious orders or to private actors through corruption, the Crown ultimately saw substantial material benefits from the Jesuit expulsion and expropriation.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: However, this windfall was delayed and did not meet some of the exaggerated expectations of vast Jesuit riches.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As noted earlier, officials had misunderstood the size and form of Jesuit holdings in part because the order had so effectively evaded oversight and taxation before 1767. 5.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Social Unrest in New Spain A second important consequence of the expulsion was social unrest.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Following the publication of the expulsion decree in 1767, a series of large revolts roiled New Spain, mostly concentrated in regions to the north of Mexico City where the Jesuit order had wielded significant influence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In addition to the material consequences, this unrest risked sparking a much larger political crisis.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In some areas, the backlash seems to have been triggered by the expulsion itself.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In San Luis de la Paz, for example, where the Jesuits held two nearby estates and a school, the secretive implementation of the expulsion order was accidentally discovered when residents confused the Crown agent charged with executing it with an undercover priest seeking to take over the town’s church.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This led to the mobilization of thousands of people—most of the town’s population—to resist the secularization of their parish and its transfer to the diocesan clergy.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These rioters were able to block the expulsion for a few days until a militia was raised to enforce the decree.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In other cases, the announcement of the expulsion served to reignite and intensify preexisting conflicts.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Starting the previous summer, a series of riots had spread across the Bajío in response to 4 In 1783, the Crown instructed that the temporalidades in the Americas remit 11,255,380 reales to pay for expulsionrelated expenses between 1767 and 1783 and 2,500,000 reales annually thereafter.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Using the nominal exchange rates in García Martínez to convert these figures into silver pesos, this corresponds to less than 60 percent of the total (and incomplete) revenue from temporalidades recorded by the Royal Treasury in New Spain between 1767 and 1821.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: 22 falling wages and increasing tax and militia burdens.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In and around the economic centers of Guanajuato and San Luis Potosí, where the Jesuits had operated numerous rural estates and urban schools, a renewed wave of unrest had started a few weeks before the proclamation of the expulsion, sparked by new regulations on weapons and vagrancy and the ordered conscriptions for newly established militias.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These disturbances intensified significantly following the announcement of expulsion.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In addition to providing a new focal point to coordinate revolt, the expulsion also reactivated local grievances over taxation, militia service, and the imposition of new royal monopolies.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In San Luis Potosí, rioters turned their anger against Spanish-born elites more generally, connecting the suppression of the Jesuits to broader grievances over other Bourbon state-building efforts.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Here and elsewhere. most of those who took part in the uprising were mining workers and commoners, but some members of the local elite were involved as well.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In Guanajuato, Castro Gutiérrez observes that prominent elites refused to assist local authorities in raising militias to suppress the rebellion, and several notables were later implicated for openly supporting the uprising.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Some mining managers even armed and instructed their workers directly to align with the rebels and defend the Jesuits (p. 156–157).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though most elites ended up collaborating with the Crown to suppress the rebellion, elite grievances over the expulsion contributed to considerable uncertainty over whether the crisis might spread further.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The extent of social unrest following the expulsion was highly uneven across the territory.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In Mexico City and Puebla, where the Jesuits had a substantial presence and where unrest would have been highly disruptive to the political leadership, the Crown concentrated its available troops in the wake of the order, successfully deterring any uprising.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Much of the unrest that did occur as a result of the expulsion was on a considerably smaller scale than the famous revolts in the mining centers of the Bajío.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: One potential example is the uptick in rural uprisings across central and southern Mexico recorded around this time.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Rural uprisings in colonial Mexico tended to be small, often confined to a single community, and they were generally brought under control within a few days.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Triggering factors highlighted in archival sources include the imposition or enforcement of taxes, labor drafts, land conflicts and other local political disputes, quarantine requirements, and internal factionalism.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Religious factors often contributed as well.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Relatively minor events, such as refurbishing the town chapel, refusing to preach in the native language, or a priest moving their place of residence, could spark revolts.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The expulsion of the Jesuits would have been disruptive to the economic, political, and religious equilibrium of many Indigenous and mestizo communities, particularly in areas of strong Jesuit presence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: To quantitatively examine how the Jesuit expulsion influenced smaller-scale uprisings, we digitize data from Taylor, who compiled a list of village rebellions using archival sources for 30 districts ( alcaldías mayores ) in central Mexico and Oaxaca from 1680 to 1810.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We match the data to 1786 administrative units to incorporate other geographic and political covariates in our analysis (see below).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We plot the total incidence of uprisings over time in the left panel of Figure 2.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though this temporal increase in rebellion appears suggestive, this was a period of many other disruptive changes across New Spain.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The formation of new militias in the 1760s, for instance, contributed to elite and popular grievances.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: During the general inspection by José de Gálvez, which set the stage for the Bourbon reforms, authorities also implemented several unpopular administrative and regulatory changes, including the increased enforcement of existing legislation, the reorganization of customs offices, the establishment of a tobacco monopoly, and an edict that facilitated debt peonage.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As noted earlier, these and related factors contributed to growing unrest before the Jesuit expulsion order in some areas, notably the Bajío.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: To more closely examine how the Jesuit expulsion influenced rural unrest, we compare the trajectory of revolt in areas with and without more direct Jesuit presence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As noted earlier, a particularly important channel of Jesuit influence in rural areas was through the ownership and management of large agricultural estates.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As a dominant employer, the Jesuits often shaped not just the local economy but also the social, religious, and political life of the communities surrounding estates.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We thus use the presence or absence of a Jesuit estate prior to 1767 as the main basis of comparison.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: To construct the dataset, we compile and digitize data on all Jesuit estates—haciendas, ranches, large grain farms, and sugar plantations—known to have been expropriated as part of the expulsion decree.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Of the 112 estates reported in de Fonseca and de Urrutia (1853), we are able to match 110 to their corresponding colonial district.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Merging with the rebellion data, 14 out of the 30 districts in Taylor’s sample include at least one Jesuit estate.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In the right panel of Figure 2, we show that the surge in uprisings following the 1767 expulsion was more pronounced in districts with Jesuit estates than in those without them, particularly in the years immediately following the expulsion.
