section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The results indicate that districts with a Jesuit estate experienced a 3 percentage point increase in the probability of unrest following the expulsion, as compared to districts without one.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: It is important to note that the overall probability of rebellion in this sample is small.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The estimated effect in fact represents a substantial relative increase, representing roughly double the pre-expulsion average incidence of rebellion (1. percent) and one-third of the within-district standard deviation in pre-expulsion unrest.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We further investigate the differential trends in rebellion in areas with and without a Jesuit estate 26 using an event-study analysis.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Figure 3 presents the point estimates and 95 percent confidence intervals (based on standard errors that are robust to serial correlation within districts and spatial correlation between districts) of an event study of the 1767 expulsion, omitting the five-year period immediately preceding the expulsion as the baseline.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This evidence suggests that the effect on unrest was particularly pronounced in the five-year period immediately following the event.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Pre-expulsion trends in rebellion appear roughly similar in areas with and without a direct Jesuit presence, lending some credibility to the parallel trends assumption in this context.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: We note, however, that the eventstudy results should be interpreted cautiously, particularly given the small number of districts in this sample and the fact that rebellion is a relatively rare event in our data.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In Online Appendix A.1, we probe the robustness of these findings under alternative assumptions and show that, while the estimated effect of the rebellion on unrest remains positive in the short term, estimates are not always statistically distinguishable from zero, and results using a smaller temporal bandwidth around 1767 are generally inconclusive. 5 Taken as a whole, both qualitative accounts and available quantitative evidence suggests that there was indeed a meaningful increase in unrest in areas affected by the Jesuit expulsion.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In addition to the well-known revolts of the Bajío, the years after the expulsion saw a rise in localized conflict in central and southern Mexico, concentrated in districts with Jesuit estates.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The unrest following the expulsion generated new and significant political and economic costs for the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As we discuss in the next section, it paradoxically also presented some new opportunities for the expansion of imperial control. 5.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Political Authority of the Crown Political unrest following the Jesuit expulsion proved damaging, but also temporary.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though undoubtedly explosive, the immediate backlash to the expulsion was ultimately contained to a few regions and quickly brought under control, thanks in part to the Crown’s expanding military power and the private militias of cooperating elites (e.g., Taylor 1979, p. 122; Brading 1994, p. 5; Tutino 5 Given the limited number of reported rebellions in the dataset and our fixed-effects empirical strategy, it is not possible to reliably estimate these event-study models on yearly data.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: See Online Appendix A.1.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Far from making concessions to defuse the crisis, officials responded with an unprecedented wave of severe repression.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Traditionally, the Crown had taken a somewhat conciliatory approach to containing unrest in Mexico.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Officials seldom resorted to collective punishment of rebelling communities and often favored mercy even when it came to the purported leaders of uprisings.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In aftermath of the 1767 uprisings, by contrast, the Crown not only meted out severe punishments to participating individuals—including a wave of executions, lashings, and sentences to exile or forced labor—it also resorted to broader strategies of repression, up to and including the abolition of municipal governments and religious institutions in rebelling communities.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Taylor characterizes the harsh approach as “the unbending response of a leading peninsular reformer [inspector-general José de Gálvez] who had little understanding of the delicate divide-and-rule policies that had governed the Mexican countryside for two centuries” (p. 122).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This heavy-handed approach could have backfired, but it did not, at least not right away.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In his efforts to contain popular unrest, Gálvez, backed by a punitive expedition, found common cause with local elites.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Under the looming threat of central coercion, many contributed resources and private militias to strengthen the Crown’s repressive apparatus, even as they privately opposed the harsh terms of the expulsion.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Gálvez and the Crown proved resilient to higher-level political defection as well.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Several well-placed colonial officials in Mexico City had attempted to undermine Gálvez’s authority in the wake of the Jesuit expulsion, in part by spreading rumors about his loyalty and morality, only to find themselves expelled back to Spain after the viceroy sided with the inspector-general and accused the dissidents of subversion against the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As Tutino notes, when faced with unrest in the immediate aftermath of the expulsion, provincial elites could have joined popular resistance against the Crown in defense of their former teachers and clergy, but they largely chose to join the “alliance for repression” led by Gálvez instead (p. 255).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The political response to the uprisings ultimately provided an opportunity for the Crown to showcase its growing strength.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: By successfully managing the unrest caused or intensified by the expulsion, the Crown demonstrated its determination and capacity to implement reforms despite significant domestic resistance, a powerful signal to opposing interests.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Bolstered by his early successes, Gálvez eventually turned his attention to broader reforms over taxation, regulation, and bureaucratic oversight.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These included, among others, the centralization of tax enforcement, the suppression of office selling, new personnel policies favoring peninsular Spaniards over creoles, a territorial reorganization of the colony through the intendancy system, and the establishment of state monopolies on the sale of products like tobacco.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These changes, collectively referred to as the Bourbon Reforms, would radically remake colonial institutions, ushering in a period of unprecedented peninsular control and increased revenue collection for the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though the Jesuit expulsion was not the first of the Bourbon reforms to be successfully implemented, historians have highlighted the crisis and response as an important watershed moment in the trajectory of colonial rule in Mexico, which would be less conciliatory and more coercive going forward.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Later structural reforms would come directly at the expense of other powerful domestic interests—including the Mexico City merchant guild, the rising creole economic elite, influential tax farmers, and the Church—but the political backlash was generally muted and easily contained.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In the words of David Brading, the Jesuit expulsion and its aftermath demonstrated that “[h]enceforth Leviathan would brook no rivals in the exercise of state power”, and this deterred others from challenging royal authority.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Somewhat less successful was the Bourbon attempt to extend state power to the northwest frontier, a region governed in large part through the Jesuits before 1767.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The seizure of Jesuit missions in the northwest helped to accelerate a pre-existing trend toward diocesan clergy control in places like 29 Sinaloa and Nayarit.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In disbanding Jesuit missions, Gálvez also expanded the administrative reach of the Crown as this implicitly reclassified mission populations as full “tribute-paying citizens” who would be in principle responsible for paying the Indigenous capitation tax.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As noted earlier, the classification of Indigenous groups in the northwest as “neophytes” outside of Crown control had been a major source of conflict between the Crown and Jesuits prior to the expulsion.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: More generally, the Jesuit expulsion provided an opportunity for reformers to revisit the longstanding approach to governing the north via distant intermediaries.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Gálvez and others saw an opportunity to fill the resulting power vacuum by replacing Jesuit missionaries with civil authorities under Crown control who could administer the territory directly.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Gálvez became personally invested in this effort, traveling to California to oversee the reallocation of mission land, the appointment of local tax officials, the creation of secular schools, and even the inventorying of supplies to be shipped up the coast from San Blas.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The results of these efforts were mixed.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Following the expulsion, a “political and military” government under Crown control was established in California for the first time.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Later, as minister of the Indies, Gálvez further created a new supreme authority to oversee the governance of the north, a Comandancia General de las Provincias Internas, and handpicked a political ally to serve in this position.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: However, Gálvez was forced to roll back and reverse some of his more ambitious state-building efforts.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: After the expulsion, longstanding conflicts against the Seris and other Indigenous groups worsened, and the Apaches took advantage of the diversion of military effort to expand attacks against newly arrived Spanish settlers and officials.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Left without their traditional intermediaries in the region, the Jesuits, the Crown was forced to bear the costs of containing these conflicts directly, with somewhat more limited success.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The Crown resorted to familiar solutions to address these problems.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: After Gálvez’s preferred hardline approach proved highly ineffective at controlling conflict in the north, he resorted to the 30 much older colonial strategy of pacification through trade, bargaining, and conciliation.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Gálvez was also forced to abandon his efforts to administer Jesuit missions directly under the Crown, eventually turning control over to a new set of intermediaries: Franciscan and (to a lesser extent) Dominican missionaries, who would take on new powers of their own.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The northwest frontier was thus never brought under solid control of the Bourbon Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As Kessell writes, “[d]espite threats by secular absolutists [.] missionaries stood out on New Spain’s contested northern frontier as boldly in the late eighteenth century as they ever had” (p. 272).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: In contrast to the experience in central Mexico, the aftermath of the Jesuit expulsion drew attention to the limits of state power along the frontier. 5.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The Unraveling of Imperial Control In the end, however, the more severe threat to Spanish rule would originate in the center, not the far north.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though officials succeeded in controlling the immediate wave of unrest that followed the Jesuit expulsion, the adverse political legacies proved to be longer-lasting.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though most local elites had sided, or at least acquiesced, with Gálvez during the unrest of 1767, the Jesuit expulsion damaged the credibility of the Crown among this critical population for reasons that were symbolic as well as material.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As noted previously, the Jesuits had often been seen as politically aligned with the creole elite.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Many elites in New Spain had been the students of Jesuit teachers, the benefactors of Jesuit institutions, and the relatives of exiled Jesuit clergy.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Jesuit clergy had openly sided with local elites against the Crown on several notable political occasions, such as during the 1624 riots in Mexico City that ended with the removal of the viceroy.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As a result of their social embeddedness, the Jesuits came to be seen as particularly “vocal and telling critics of European ethnocentrism [.] whose expulsion typified Spanish colonial tyranny”.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: After 1767, some expelled Jesuit scholars and clergy, many of whom were Mexican by birth, became advocates of Mexican culture and interests while in exile.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: One notable example is the Mexican-born Jesuit historian Francisco Javier Clavijero, whose influential writing would extol 31 the Aztec civilization as “greatly superior to that which the Phoenicians and Carthaginians found in our Spain” and the Nahuatl language as “as euphonious a language as German or Polish”. In addition to bolstering patriotic sentiment, these writings served to reinforce the popular representation of the Jesuit expulsion as an example of peninsular overreach at the expense of creole—and more specifically Mexican —interests.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Through these and other writings, Jesuit priests helped to establish an intellectual movement in exile that contributed to the foundation of a “Mexican” national identity.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Some Jesuit scholars later argued that their expulsion had eliminated an important barrier to the spread of liberal ideas across colonial Mexico on the eve of independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though direct grievances surrounding the expulsion would subside with time, these longer-term political and cultural consequences would prove important.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Political tensions with the creole elite persisted and periodically arose during conflicts with the Crown over other matters.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The latent conflict became newly salient at the turn of the nineteenth century when a series of imperial crises severely tested creole elites’ loyalties to the Crown.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Facing a fiscal crisis in Spain, royal authorities attempted a series of highly unpopular reforms to raise revenue across the Empire.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These efforts included the consolidation of royal bonds, leading to the expropriation of resources from educational, religious, and social institutions across the colony, harming both local elites and Indigenous populations.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This effort also forced wealthy colonial elites to make loans and donations to the Crown to raise revenue.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The crisis deepened in 1808 after Napoleon invaded Spain, ousting the king from power and precipitating an attempted coup against the viceroy in Mexico City.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: That same year, a major drought sparked a subsistence crisis in the countryside, raising the threat of a larger political rupture.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Pre-exisiting elite and popular grievances made it difficult for the Crown to weather these shocks and contributed to the crisis growing out of control.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This wave of unrest culminated in the outbreak of the Hidalgo Revolt in 1810, which began Mexico’s War of Independence, a series of related 32 conflicts that led to the end of Spanish colonial rule after 300 years.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Historians and commentators have long linked the expulsion of the Jesuits to the later movement for Mexican independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Hubert Bancroft (1885), for example, suggests that “the expulsion in 1767 of the Jesuits, who had ingratiated themselves in the hearts of the lower orders, insulted the people in their dearest affections [.] [f]rom that time conspiracy arose and became widespread.” (p. 17).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Brian Hamnett similarly writes that “[v]iolent popular reaction combined with elite disapproval to turn the task of expulsion into a bitter affair, the memory of which lasted through to the revolution of 1810” (p. 266).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The legacies of the Jesuit expulsion on shaping the political consciousness and grievances of the creole elite during the conflict have been emphasized in other work as well, particularly as focused on the early wave of the conflict in the Bajío region where Jesuit influence had been strong.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Tellingly, many local leaders during the War of Independence were educated members of the creole elite, including many parish priests Taylor.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Most notably, Miguel Hidalgo— who led the first and largest of the insurrections beginning the war—had studied under the Jesuits at the College of San Francisco Javier and had experienced the disruption of the expulsion first-hand.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Another indication that the political legacies of 1767 were salient during the years of the conflict was that the reinstatement of the Jesuit order remained an active political interest of the creole elite.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Within Iberia, the overwhelming majority of creole deputies from Spanish America (29 out of 30) voted in favor of restoring the Jesuits in the Cortes of Cádiz in 1812, though the measure was ultimately defeated by strong opposition from peninsular deputies.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Back in the Americas, the reinstatement of the Jesuits was among the policies approved, though never implemented, by the insurgent Congress of Chilpancingo in 1813.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: At this congress, the prominent insurgent leader José María Morelos is reported to have said “I sincerely love the Jesuits, and although I did not study with them, I understand that reinstating them is necessary”.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Though there is broad recognition that the Jesuit expulsion played a role in the War of Independence, some debate remains over the extent and specific mechanisms of its influence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The connection between the Jesuit expulsion and the independence movement is made murkier by the Jesuits’ shifting political circumstances in the years around Independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The order was briefly and partially reinstated in 1816 and aligned with the royalist camp until independence, later dissolving again in 1821.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This ambiguous turn of events created a strong incentive for the Jesuits’ supporters to later emphasize the order’s patriotic connections when lobbying for the order’s return in the early decades of independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These later representations likely shaped the historical memory surrounding the events of independence, though, as Zermeño Padilla notes, the order’s liberal detractors also recognized the Jesuits’ bonds with the local population and their significance to colonial education even as they criticized this influence. 6 Building on the sizable historical literature on this case, we take a closer look at the connection between the Jesuit expulsion and the outbreak of the the War of Independence quantitatively, focusing on regional variation in the course of the conflict within Mexico.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: To do this, we use data from Ortiz Escamilla on insurgent activity during the war (1810–1821).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Ortiz Escamilla uses archival sources to identify insurgent localities, along with an approximate date of insurgent activities and, when known, its local leaders.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The main outcome variable we examine is the number of insurgency groups in a given district (i.e., the 1786 administrative unit). 7 We examine the conditional correlations between insurgent activity and two measures of exposure to Jesuit influence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The first measure is whether the district contained a Jesuit estate as of 1767 using the de Fonseca and de Urrutia (1853) data described earlier.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These services, being easily recognized by all—unlike their drawbacks, which were apparent to only a few—won them the esteem of the people”. 7 In Appedix Table A.4, we present results restricting attention to insurgent groups emerging prior to the Jesuit’s brief return in 1816.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits

text: These include the mean and standard deviation of drought conditions in 1808 as measured by the Palmer Drought Severity Index, potential agricultural maize suitability (from the FAO), log elevation and area of the district, log distance to Mexico, and the latitude and longitude of the district centroid.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Looking first at Jesuit estates, the coefficient estimates are generally positive, indicating that these areas experienced somewhat more insurgent activity during the conflict than other areas.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These coefficient estimates are positive, larger in magnitude, and statistically significant at conventional levels in all specifications.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The estimates indicate that districts containing a Jesuit school as of 1767 saw on average 3–4 more insurgent groups during the conflict as compared to others.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: This magnitude is stable across specifications, corresponding to about three-quarters to a full standard deviation of the outcome variable. 10 This cross-sectional evidence is broadly consistent with historical work on Mexico’s War of Independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Elite divisions and grievances—including the lingering legacies of the Jesuit expulsion— shaped the elite’s willingness to support the Crown and suppress the insurgency.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: These fractures, in turn, influenced commoners’ incentives to join the rebellion, driving the conflict’s spread across the territory among Indigenous and mestizo communities.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As the qualitative literature also emphasizes, the connection between popular grievances over the Jesuit expulsion and insurgency was complicated, which might explain the null finding on 9 In Appendix Figure A.3, we plot the incidence of revolts around the onset of the Hidalgo Revolt, distinguishing between districts with and without pre-1767 Jesuit estates.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Unsurprisingly, unrest increases overall after 1810.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Moreover, this increase is somewhat larger in districts that previously held Jesuit-controlled estates. 10 In Appendix Table A.3, we examine an alternative outcome, a binary indicator for whether any insurgent activity took place in the district as an alternative outcome.

text: These estimates are positive and of a similar magnitude but have much larger standard errors given the small number of observations and are not generally statistically significant.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The expulsion had an important effect on the political consciousness of non-elites and Indigenous groups as well, particularly through its impact on the Mexican church and popular Catholicism.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: However, there is less documentary evidence on how these factors contributed commoner participation in the insurgency, if at all.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: One reason for this may be that less documentation exists on the motivations of commoners and Indigenous groups relative to the elite political leadership.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Another might be that variation in commoner participation in the conflict is better explained by localized concerns or factors rather than national-level trends.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As we discuss earlier, the upheaval triggered by the expulsion in 1767 often provided an opportunity for commoners to mobilize over other, unrelated causes.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: As Tutino argues, even in places where rioters explicitly cited the treatment of Jesuits in mobilization, such as in San Luis de la Paz, the appropriation of this cause can be seen as a strategic attempt to bring elites on board with popular appeals over other matters (p. 247).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: While uncertainty surrounding the impending expropriation of Jesuit estates provided a structural opening for popular rebellion in 1767, it less clear that the much-earlier seizure of Jesuit properties would have a similar effect in 1810 given that many of these estates simply continued to operate under new ownership.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: There are also reasons to believe any impact of the Jesuit expulsion on popular participation in the insurgency may not be captured by the cross-sectional evidence on the earlier presence of Jesuit estates.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: There are clear symbolic continuities between the post-1767 wave of uprisings across the Bajío and the Hidalgo revolt of 1810, for example, which originated in the same region.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The expulsion also had important indirect effects on peasant mobilization through the stress that it placed on colonial institutions elsewhere.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Tutino, for example, cites the example of guerrilla conflict in the Sierra Gorda during and after the War of Independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: While there was little, if any, direct Jesuit presence in this region, local Franciscan missionaries (famously including Junipero Serra) were dispatched to the northwest frontier to fill the power vacuum left by the Jesuit expulsion, which then left the Crown ill-equipped to address later conflicts in the areas 37 that missionaries had vacated (p. 198–9).

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Other work has illustrated how the Crown’s broader efforts to secularize missions and parishes, affecting not just the Jesuits but the other regular orders as well, contributed to undermining the legitimacy of colonial rule in places like the Chapala region of Jalisco, creating fertile ground for later insurgent activity.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: Taken as a whole, the evidence in this section suggests an interesting and multifaceted connection between the Jesuit expulsion and the outbreak of the War of Independence.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: There was more insurgent activity in areas where Jesuit schools had been operational prior to 1767, and considerable qualitative evidence ties the Jesuit expulsion to patterns of mobilization and participation in the later crisis.

section_name: The Consequences of the Expulsion of the Jesuits
text: The Jesuit expulsion—which had promised to bolster the political and economic control of the Crown at the expense of intermediaries—-thus contributed to the collapse of Spanish rule. 6.

section_name: Conclusion
text: This paper has examined the causes and consequences of the expulsion of the Jesuit order from New Spain in 1767.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The Jesuits had been important allies and intermediaries of the Spanish Crown in the consolidation of colonial rule, but they also represented a threat to power.

section_name: Conclusion
text: As religious authorities, the Jesuits could claim legitimacy from a higher power than the king himself.

section_name: Conclusion
text: They also controlled considerable resources and commanded a great deal of respect across different segments of colonial society as a function of their roles as educators, missionaries, and employers.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The same characteristics that had made the Jesuits uniquely useful partners to the Crown eventually made them uniquely threatening to imperial interests.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Through the 1767 expulsion, the Crown solved some problems for itself while creating others.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The direct threat of Jesuit subversion disappeared virtually overnight as Jesuit missionaries, scholars, clergy, and estate operators were sent into exile.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The seizure of Jesuits’ assets eventually provided a financial windfall for the Crown, albeit a somewhat smaller one than had been imagined in anticipation.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Long-running conflicts between Jesuit clergy and the Crown over taxation, political jurisdiction, and religious authority were rendered moot, providing an opportunity to extend state authority into new regions and issue areas.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The successful execution of the expulsion and the 38 Crown’s swift suppression of the brief unrest that followed signaled its resolve and may have helped deter resistance to the next wave of Bourbon reforms.

section_name: Conclusion
text: At the same time, the sudden expulsion represented a costly and risky political strategy.

section_name: Conclusion
text: In expelling their erstwhile intermediaries, the Crown was forced to pick up the direct and indirect costs of educating elites, providing social services, and pacifying conflict, and they were not always well-equipped to handle these duties.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The political upheaval of the expulsion further created an opening for a damaging, if short-lived, wave of social unrest.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Even as the immediate crisis was contained, over the longer term, the popular and elite grievances over the expulsion would contribute to the outbreak of the War of Independence and the eventual collapse of Spanish rule.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Though Spanish authorities had long feared the Jesuits’ foreign influence, the colonial state was eventually defeated from within.

section_name: Conclusion
text: The centuries-long evolution of Jesuit influence in New Spain illustrates a general lesson about the benefits and costs of institutional partnerships between the state and religious intermediaries.

section_name: Conclusion
text: By formally aligning with religious authorities and institutions as intermediaries, political rulers can borrow the ready-made legitimacy and resources associated with religion to extend the state’s power.

section_name: Conclusion
text: At the same time, there is an underlying “institutional incoherence” in these arrangements (Faguet TBD).

section_name: Conclusion
text: When religious intermediaries later come in conflict with the state, they become especially dangerous opponents, not only because of their independent legitimacy or resources, but because of the new and deeper connections with society forged as a result of their work as intermediaries.

section_name: Conclusion
text: This tension makes it difficult to roll back institutional dependence on religious intermediaries once established, limiting the autonomy of the state to extend its control without risking a larger rupture.
